chapters.title: Social justice — a utopia worth fighting for
chapters.text:
The ongoing fight for social justice has been a focal point for quite some time. Sadly, many still struggle with defining, understanding, and accepting it, and there are as many opponents as supporters. Since society has misinterpreted this idea, ridiculed it, and dismissed it so much, many activists are wary of calling themselves social justice advocates. Let's break down this phenomenon to move past these preconceived negative notions.

How does social fairness work? In simple terms, it's when everyone has a chance to succeed, regardless of background. This movement aims to educate and inform people to promote crucial changes. Advocates of this idea can focus on different aspects, including gender justice, racial justice, and others. But in the end, all of them have the same goal — equity.

Equity and equality concepts may seem similar but differ in practice. The first means being fair and unbiased, while the second is equality in rights, opportunities, and status.

When we apply both terms to society and justice, we see how critical it is to differentiate between them. The idea of social equity emphasizes access to success (money, education, happiness), while social equality focuses more on the fair distribution of success so everyone has the same wealth and enjoyment. Social justice differs from socialism; it is not about everyone having the same wealth or happiness. Instead, it is about removing barriers to various individuals and their access to social resources.

Social justice advocates aim to educate and inform so that institutions will be more inclusive.

While an equitable society may seem like a pipedream for some, social justice advocates believe we can achieve it. Even if we don't reach social equity, the difference people make along the way can make the lives of oppressed groups easier. So, get ready to dive into the issue of social equality and gain knowledge that will help everyone live together in harmony.

chapters.title: The cycle of oppression: the main obstacle to equity
chapters.text:
A happy social life relies heavily on justice. Without it, people cannot trust each other or build relationships. Achieving justice restores balance, fosters trust, and creates a harmonious social life. But what keeps us from establishing a fair society? The answer lies in the pervasive phenomenon of oppression.

Oppression is closely related to social power, which means the ability to control resources, influence people and make decisions. Pressure occurs when one group of people has more power than another, resulting in an unequal distribution of resources and privileges. Due to its cyclical nature, oppression is hard to fight.

Let's break down the oppression cycle to gain a better understanding. It all starts with spotting differences - whether in how people look, how they act, or what they believe. We then associate these differences with certain groups, like race, nationality, or gender. This way of viewing people through a particular lens leads us to form stereotypes, setting the stage for the next phase of oppression.

Stereotypes are generalized beliefs and opinions about a particular person or group. They are always damaging and serve as the basis for prejudice — negative or limiting beliefs about a group of people. Conscious or unconscious bias is a form of discrimination, the penultimate step to oppression.

People discriminate against others when they use their authority or power to limit someone's opportunities. While discrimination occurs individually, oppression happens on a societal or institutional level. It's like when businesses refuse to hire someone based purely on race, gender identity, or sexual orientation. In some cases, laws even support such discriminatory actions.

The cycle continues through internalization. Those who belong to the oppressed part of society grow up with unjust stereotypes and prejudices about their group. As a result, they act to support those beliefs, no matter how damaging they are. For example, some girls grow up hearing that they are bad at sports, so they don't try to be good at them, accepting the stereotype. They fail because they don't try, thus creating a “second generation” of stereotypes.

Privilege is a harmful side effect of oppression — persecuted groups face obstacles, while those in dominion gain extra opportunities.

Though the cycle may seem impossible to break, it isn't. The trick is to start with the little things, such as dismantling stereotypes and focusing on education.

chapters.title: We are more than just a label
chapters.text:
Lately, there have been many conversations about different identities — racial, ethnic, gender, and many others. But how do we know what our identity is? Putting a label on everything can make navigating a complex society easier but can also be dangerous and damaging.

Humans are way too complex to be fully represented or described by just one label. Sure, we can learn a lot from someone saying, “I identify as a woman,” but will we understand that person based on just one aspect? Definitely not, because this is just one detail that tells very little about a person.

Humans are many different things at the same time. Essentially, we are a complex mash-up of hundreds of identities that come together to make one distinct self. Race, gender, ethnicity, sexuality, religion, career, and socioeconomic status are some identities a person can possess. Add them all up, and you'll get yourself — an exceptional individual.

You can't understand someone else deeply unless you eliminate stereotypes and generalizations.

Humans tend to simplify difficult things, and as a result, we are reduced to one single label that doesn't even begin to cover all of the amazing things we are. Even though stereotypes hurt, this simplification isn't all bad. It shows that despite all our differences, we have something in common that allows us to connect and understand each other. It gives us a sense of belonging and the opportunity to be part of something bigger, which is also necessary for a fulfilling life.

Being familiar with someone's identity differs from knowing their story, background, or religion. Therefore, it is best to connect with the person as a whole rather than just one aspect of them. It is the only way to build close relationships and feel unity with someone.

Every time we call someone a misogynist, there’s a good chance we’re creating a roadblock on our path to gender equity and social justice. ~ Sam Killermann

Of course, we are not the same, but we are not as different as we might initially think. Our similarities are the hope for building a more fair and compassionate society.

chapters.title: Gender roles and how they affect personality
chapters.text:
Among all the different identities, the one that consistently remains at the center of attention is gender identity. It defines and shapes our everyday lives and controls our every step. It includes the norms and roles that are an integral part of our social lives.

Gender norms are informal rules and guidelines society imposes based on a person’s sex. They are inescapable and deeply rooted in our minds. They dictate what to wear, how to look, and behave. “Are you a boy? Pink's not your color, pal. Are you a girl? Then stick to playing with dolls.” That's the sort of messaging gender norms convey in our society. They may change over time, but their overt and covert influence prevails.

Social norms are mostly informal, but we feel extreme pressure to comply with them.

Gender roles are another social and cultural concept that underlies societal anticipations. These are gender norms united in certain groups. “Wife,” “husband,” “mother,” and “father” are examples of such roles that define our traits and shape society's expectations of our behavior. Figuratively speaking, gender roles are a tree, and gender norms are its branches.

Many people get stuck in an insurmountable problem with gender roles: conforming to them causes internal conflict, and disregarding them causes societal disagreement. But this doesn't mean that the goal of a social justice advocate is to tear apart the entire concept of gender roles. In fact, the latter can be a supportive and helpful element of society. They are crucial for understanding ourselves and other people. Though restrictive for many, they provide a common language to explore gender and identities. So, what is the ultimate goal? It's all about letting people embrace their gender and express it in ways that feel true to them without society calling the shots. By separating gender from gender roles, we can work toward achieving gender equity.

Upon closer examination, we see that society dictates gender norms, not biology or nature. And if they are, in fact, not biological imperatives, they can be changed and uprooted. The old way of thinking about gender and gender roles no longer works because people don't fit into rigid boxes. Therefore, we need a new approach to understanding gender and addressing related issues.

Did you know? The term “gender” was first introduced in science by American psychoanalyst Robert Stoller.

chapters.title: A more nuanced way of visualizing gender
chapters.text:
For easier understanding, we can divide gender into identity, expression, and anatomical sex. These elements are interrelated but not interconnected.

Gender identity is how we conform to specific gender criteria. It is all about how we perceive and think about ourselves. A person can have issues if their understanding of themselves doesn't match the gender ascribed to them at birth.

Gender self-expression is how we present ourselves in society and how others perceive us depending on previous expectations. People can represent gender through clothing, behavior, and other socialization aspects.

Anatomical sex refers to the physical traits we are born with — organs, hormones, and chromosomes. We have come to think of this as straightforward and binary when the reality is far from simple. There are many nuances in assigning someone's sex at birth. For instance, it's possible to be born with a woman's internal reproductive system but with a man's genitalia. In North America, the total number of people whose anatomy differs from the standard is 1 in 100.

As we can see, all elements of gender are complex and nuanced. So, to explain it better, people often use spectrums. For example, they might express their gender identity in a “from woman to man” spectrum. But many people claim that their individuality falls outside this binary scheme — they can identify as both, neither, or something completely different. Because of this, using ranges to define gender identity, expression, and sex doesn't always work. They leave no room for those who do not recognize themself in the spectrum categories.

There is a mistaken conviction in our society that to be more of one, we need to be less of the other. But, in reality, we can have both.

In his “-Ness” model, Sam Killermann proposes to visualize gender more subtly. According to this perspective, “man” and “woman,” although different terms, are not necessarily opposites. Two different sequences represent the “-Ness” model, with a zero on one end and a specific concept on another. Using this model, a person can demonstrate that they are a lot of both or neither of two different ideas. It adds more intricacy and helps us escape the traditional binary system.

chapters.title: Let’s leave “the binary” to computers
chapters.text:
A binary understanding of gender is deeply embedded in our society, norms, and rules. It is rigid and restrictive, lacking nuance and compassion. Living in such a reality when, deep down, you feel like you don't fit in can cause identity dissonance and hurtful internal conflicts. The dissonance that gender restrictions cause can lead to depression, anxiety, and other dangerous mental illnesses.

Moving away from a male-or-female perspective may be liberating and even therapeutic. It allows us to explore gender in a more open-minded and inclusive way.

Two options for gender is too few. There are billions of people on this earth. We owe ourselves at least that many choices for how to be a person. ~ Sam Killermann

Gender is an inherently social construction, a means of classifying people without knowing their whole story. It is also a concept that is difficult to define and describe. Killermann claims that every person he has ever talked to has a different understanding of gender and what it means to be “male,” “female,” or somewhere in between. We classify traits into “male” and “female” differently based on our upbringing, experiences, and other facets of our identities. Doesn't this mean that we are all at least a bit genderqueer? If that's the case, the norm doesn't exist, and queerness is no longer queer. Understanding this can help us become more empathetic toward each other's experiences.

What can we do to spread awareness of gender issues in a way that won’t cause more damage? The best method is to be careful and intentional with what you say and where. While explaining complex concepts and describing the drawbacks of the binary gender system, remember the following tips:

• Avoid using “I” and “You” statements.
• Don’t talk down or lash out.
• Target the problem and problematic behavior, not the person.
• Pick and choose your battles — not everyone is ready to listen, and that’s okay.

Achieving social justice is a long process, so we must keep trying even when things don't work.

The most crucial thing in the fight for social justice is to remain kind and humane without backtracking and abandoning your convictions.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
The simple truths are that:

1. We are all different and just as much the same.
2. The way others perceive us matters.

We all care to some extent. We cherish our relationships, take pride in them, and strengthen each other through social ties. Consequently, regardless of gender, ethnicity, or sexual orientation, we should all aim for a society in which everyone is secure, educated, healthy, and understood. We must do all we can to take a leap of faith and begin our journey toward this attainable Utopia.

Our society is in the midst of a large-scale irreversible gender revolution. What each of us does now will influence the outcome.

It's a good idea to put a new spin on the Golden Rule, “Do unto others as you would have them do unto you,” since it's inherently damaging. This moral law assumes everyone wants to be treated the same way, but in reality, we all have different needs. So, “Do unto others as they would have done unto them” is a universal vision that will help us stay compassionate and understanding.

Asking others how they want to be treated, listening to them, and accepting how they express their many identities is a sure way forward.

Try this


1. Draw a “-Ness” model and determine where you fall regarding your gender identity, gender expression, and anatomical sex. The result may be surprising.
2. If you are comfortable with it, share the ideas of this summary with other people in your life. It will help bring more awareness to the issues of gender and oppression.

Read it till the end?
